title: Knowledge Sharing and Threat Briefing Format
content:
## Purpose

This procedure establishes the format and cadence for knowledge sharing activities within the SOC, including daily threat briefings, IOC sharing processes, and team knowledge updates. Consistent knowledge sharing ensures all analysts are aware of current threats, recent incidents, and new detection techniques, reducing information silos and improving collective response capability.

## Scope

Applies to all SOC analysts, threat intelligence analysts, and detection engineers. Covers daily threat briefings, weekly team updates, incident knowledge articles, and IOC distribution processes.

## Prerequisites

- SOC collaboration channel (Teams/Slack) with dedicated threat-intel and knowledge-sharing channels
- Access to threat intelligence platforms and feeds
- SOC knowledge base or wiki accessible to all team members
- Weekly meeting slot reserved for team knowledge sharing

## Procedure

### Daily Threat Briefing

**Frequency**: Every business day, posted by 09:00 local time
**Responsible**: Threat Intelligence Analyst or designated daily briefer (rotating role)
**Audience**: All SOC analysts
**Channel**: #soc-threat-intel Slack/Teams channel + email distribution list

**Daily Threat Briefing Template:**

```
=== DAILY THREAT BRIEFING ===
Date: [YYYY-MM-DD]
Prepared by: [Name]
Classification: TLP:AMBER (internal use only)

--- TOP STORIES ---

1. [Headline]
   Source: [Vendor advisory / CISA / news source]
   Relevance: [How this affects our organization — HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW]
   Summary: [2-3 sentences describing the threat]
   Action: [What the SOC should do — monitor for IOCs, verify patches, etc.]

2. [Headline]
   Source: [Source]
   Relevance: [HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW]
   Summary: [2-3 sentences]
   Action: [Recommended action]

3. [Headline]
   Source: [Source]
   Relevance: [HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW]
   Summary: [2-3 sentences]
   Action: [Recommended action]

--- NEW VULNERABILITIES ---

| CVE | Product | CVSS | Exploited in Wild | Our Exposure | Action |
|-----|---------|------|------------------|--------------|--------|
| CVE-2026-XXXX | [Product] | 9.8 | Yes | [Yes/No/Checking] | [Patch/Monitor/N/A] |
| CVE-2026-YYYY | [Product] | 7.5 | No | Yes | Patch within SLA |

--- NEW IOCs ---

(See IOC Sharing Format section below for details)
[Count] new IOCs added to blocklists/watchlists today.
Source: [Campaign/advisory name]

--- ACTIVE CAMPAIGNS ---

[Brief summary of any ongoing threat campaigns relevant to our
industry or technology stack]

--- SOC INTERNAL ---

- [Detection rule changes deployed overnight]
- [New playbook or runbook published]
- [Tool maintenance or outage notices]
- [Training opportunities or resources]

--- YESTERDAY'S NOTABLE INCIDENTS ---

[1-2 sentence summary of any significant incidents from the
previous day, with case IDs for reference]
```

### IOC Sharing Format

When sharing IOCs internally (from threat intelligence, incident investigations, or external feeds), use this standardized format:

```
=== IOC REPORT ===
Report ID: IOC-[YYYY]-[sequential number]
Date: [YYYY-MM-DD]
Source: [Threat intel feed / Incident investigation / External advisory]
Threat: [Malware family / Campaign name / Threat actor]
TLP: [WHITE/GREEN/AMBER/RED]
Confidence: [High/Medium/Low]

--- INDICATORS ---

IP Addresses:
| IP | Context | First Seen | Last Seen | Action |
|----|---------|-----------|----------|--------|
| 198.51.100.42 | C2 server | 2026-02-10 | 2026-02-14 | Block at FW |
| 203.0.113.55 | Exfiltration endpoint | 2026-02-12 | 2026-02-14 | Block at FW |

Domains:
| Domain | Context | Action |
|--------|---------|--------|
| evil-c2.example.com | C2 domain | Block at DNS + proxy |
| phish-login.example.net | Credential phishing | Block at email GW + proxy |

File Hashes (SHA-256):
| Hash | File Name | Malware Family | Action |
|------|----------|---------------|--------|
| a1b2c3d4e5... | update.exe | Cobalt Strike beacon | Block in EDR |
| f6g7h8i9j0... | invoice.docm | Macro dropper | Block in email GW + EDR |

URLs:
| URL | Context | Action |
|-----|---------|--------|
| https://evil.example.com/payload | Payload download | Block at proxy |

Email Addresses:
| Email | Context | Action |
|-------|---------|--------|
| attacker@phish.example.com | Phishing sender | Block at email GW |

--- MITRE ATT&CK MAPPING ---
[Techniques observed in this campaign]

--- DETECTION GUIDANCE ---
[Specific SIEM queries, EDR hunt queries, or behavioral indicators
that can detect this threat beyond simple IOC matching]

--- REFERENCES ---
[Links to advisories, reports, or analysis]
```

**IOC Distribution Process:**

1. Threat intelligence analyst formats IOCs using the template above.
2. IOCs are posted to #soc-threat-intel channel for analyst awareness.
3. Actionable IOCs (High confidence) are simultaneously pushed to:
   - Firewall/proxy blocklists (IPs, domains)
   - EDR global block (file hashes)
   - Email gateway block (sender domains, email addresses)
   - SIEM watchlist (all IOC types for detection)
   - DNS resolver blocklist (domains)
4. Medium confidence IOCs are added to SIEM watchlists for monitoring only (not blocking).
5. Low confidence IOCs are documented but not deployed until validation.

### Weekly Knowledge Sharing Session

**Frequency**: Weekly, 30-45 minutes
**Audience**: All SOC team members
**Format**: Rotating presentations by team members

**Session Types (rotate weekly):**

1. **Incident Deep Dive**: One analyst presents a recent interesting investigation — the alert, the analysis process, the findings, and the lessons learned. This is the most valuable knowledge sharing format.

2. **Technique of the Week**: An analyst or threat intel specialist presents a specific MITRE ATT&CK technique, including how it works, how to detect it, real-world examples, and relevant detection rules.

3. **Tool Tip**: A team member demonstrates a feature, query, or workflow in one of the SOC tools that other analysts may not be aware of.

4. **Threat Actor Profile**: The threat intelligence team presents a profile of a specific threat actor or group, including their TTPs, targeting, and how to detect their activity.

5. **Detection Rule Review**: The detection engineering team walks through new or modified detection rules, explaining the logic, expected false positive rate, and triage guidance.

**Session Template:**
```
Presenter: [Name]
Date: [Date]
Topic Type: [Incident Deep Dive / Technique / Tool / Threat Actor / Detection]
Title: [Descriptive title]

Key Takeaways:
1. [Main point]
2. [Main point]
3. [Main point]

Action Items:
- [What analysts should do differently based on this knowledge]

Resources:
- [Links to relevant documentation, tools, or references]
```

### Knowledge Base Article Standards

When an investigation reveals new techniques, tools, or procedures not covered by existing documentation, the investigating analyst should create a knowledge base article:

**When to create a KB article:**
- New malware variant encountered that required novel analysis techniques
- A detection gap was identified and a new detection method was developed
- A common analyst question was answered through investigation
- A new tool or technique was used successfully for the first time
- An external advisory required a custom response procedure

**KB Article Template:**
```
Title: [Descriptive title]
Author: [Name]
Date: [Date]
Category: [Malware Analysis / Detection / Investigation Technique / Tool Usage / Threat Intel]
Tags: [Relevant tags for searchability]

## Summary
[1-2 paragraphs summarizing what this article covers and why it is useful]

## Background
[Context: what prompted this article — the incident, advisory, or question]

## Technical Details
[Detailed explanation with screenshots, queries, code samples, or
command-line examples as appropriate]

## Detection
[How to detect this threat/technique — SIEM queries, EDR indicators,
behavioral patterns]

## Response
[How to respond if this is encountered — triage steps, containment
actions, eradication procedures]

## References
[Links to external resources, related KB articles, or vendor documentation]
```

### Knowledge Sharing Metrics

Track these metrics to ensure the knowledge sharing program is active and effective:

- Daily threat briefings published on schedule: Target 100%
- Weekly knowledge sharing sessions conducted: Target > 90%
- KB articles created per month: Target > 4
- IOC reports distributed per week: Track volume and source
- Analyst participation in knowledge sharing sessions: Track attendance

## Escalation Criteria

- Critical threat intelligence requiring immediate action by all analysts: Post to #soc-urgent channel with @here notification; do not wait for the daily briefing
- Knowledge gap identified that poses operational risk (no analyst knows how to handle a specific scenario): Escalate to SOC Manager for training prioritization
- Vendor-provided intelligence under TLP:RED restrictions: Distribute only to named individuals per TLP protocol

## References

- Traffic Light Protocol (TLP) v2.0 — FIRST
- MITRE ATT&CK Framework — Enterprise Matrix
- STIX/TAXII Standards for Threat Intelligence Sharing
- NIST SP 800-150 — Guide to Cyber Threat Information Sharing
- Organization's Information Sharing and Analysis Policy
